title: Setting up a home papermaking studio, space, water, and equipment
subtitle: What you actually need before pulling your first sheet
excerpt: Home papermaking needs water, drainage, a flat surface, and enough space to lay wet sheets flat. It does not require a dedicated room. A kitchen or laundry area with outdoor access for the vat serves adequately. This article covers the practical setup for a working home papermaking space.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923): Hunter describes in detail the layout and requirements of a small paper studio based on his own experience establishing a working mill for hand papermaking in the early 20th century. This tutorial draws on his account of the minimum viable space for small-batch sheet pulling.

glossaryTerms.definition: The traditional name for the person who pulls sheets in a paper mill, the skilled worker at the vat.

content.text: The traditional paper mill organised work around three stations: the vat (where sheets were pulled), the couching mound (where wet sheets were transferred to felts), and the drying room. Home papermaking compresses all three into a single workspace. The requirements are modest, water supply, drainage, and a flat surface for glass-drying, and a kitchen or utility room meets all of them.

The vat is filled from a tap and drained into a sink or bucket. Papermaking generates substantial volumes of very dilute water (mostly water, a small amount of fibre). This can go down a standard drain, the fibres are short and soft, unlike the long fibres that block household drains in textile work. Do not pour pulp down a drain directly from the blender, always dilute heavily first.

The vat needs to be large enough that the mould can be inserted diagonally and levelled within the water without hitting the sides. For an A4 mould (297 × 210 mm), a vat with internal dimensions of at least 400 × 320 mm and 250 mm depth is the minimum. A 60-litre storage box or a deep washing-up bowl works at home scale. Place it at a comfortable height of 80-90 cm from the floor to avoid the
bend that causes back pain in extended sessions.

The blender for pulp preparation should be near a sink but kept away from the vat, fibre splatter is inevitable when loading and rinsing the blender. A dedicated blender stored separately from food-preparation equipment is essential. Label it clearly.

Glass-dried sheets need a clean, smooth surface in a location where they won't be disturbed for several hours. A tile or glass pane left on a counter is sufficient for small batches. For larger batches, a board on sawhorses covered with Perspex or glass can dry 20 or more sheets simultaneously. Avoid direct sunlight during drying, it causes uneven contraction and buckling.

A basic home setup needs: a mould and deckle sized to the paper, a vat, a blender, six felts for couching, two flat pressing boards, a weight, and a glass or tile surface for drying. Commercial mould-and-deckle sets come from papermaking suppliers. A home-built frame from timber batten and window screen is a lower-cost option.
